learning_objectives: Write Python scripts for security automation tasks
Understand data types, control flow, and functions
Use Python standard library modules relevant to security

lecture_notes.introduction: Python is the language of cybersecurity. From scripting exploit development to automating incident response, Python proficiency is essential for every security professional.
key_concepts: Python data types: strings, bytes, lists, dictionaries
Control flow: conditionals, loops, exception handling
Functions and modular code organization
Standard library: hashlib, json, pathlib, struct
Virtual environments and package management with pip
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a script that hashes all files in a directory tree with SHA-256 and compares them to a known-good baseline for integrity checking

review.value: Security work frequently involves raw bytes (network packets, binary files). Python 3 distinguishes between str (text) and bytes (raw data). Use encode()/decode() to convert.
Standard library module for cryptographic hashing. hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest() produces a hex digest. Used for file integrity, password hashing foundations, and IoC comparison.

learning_objectives: Create TCP and UDP client/server programs
Parse and construct network packets with Scapy

lecture_notes.introduction: Network programming enables you to build custom security tools for scanning, sniffing, and communicating with network services programmatically.
key_concepts: Socket programming: TCP client, TCP server, UDP communication
Scapy: packet crafting, sending, and sniffing
Building a port scanner with socket connections
Raw packet manipulation for custom protocol analysis
Asynchronous networking for performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a multi-threaded port scanner and a Scapy-based ARP scanner that discovers all hosts on the local network

review.value: TCP uses SOCK_STREAM with connect(), send(), recv(). UDP uses SOCK_DGRAM with sendto(), recvfrom(). Always handle timeouts and connection errors.
Scapy builds packets layer by layer. sr1() sends and receives one response. Enables custom scanning, fuzzing, and protocol testing.

learning_objectives: Automate web interactions with the requests library
Parse HTML and JSON responses for security data extraction
Build scripts that interact with security tool APIs

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern security operations rely heavily on APIs. Automating interactions with web applications and security tool APIs is a critical skill for SOC analysts and security engineers.
key_concepts: HTTP requests: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE with the requests library
Session management and authentication handling
JSON parsing and response validation
API interaction: VirusTotal, Shodan, AbuseIPDB
Rate limiting and error handling for API clients
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a script that takes a list of IP addresses, checks each against VirusTotal and AbuseIPDB APIs, and produces a consolidated threat report

review.value: Most security APIs use API keys in headers. Some use OAuth2. Never hardcode API keys; use environment variables or config files.
APIs enforce request limits. Implement exponential backoff on 429 responses. Batch requests where possible. Cache results to avoid redundant queries.

learning_objectives: Build scripts for log analysis and pattern detection

lecture_notes.introduction: Security analysts frequently need to parse binary files, analyze logs, and extract artifacts programmatically.
key_concepts: Binary file reading: struct module for unpacking binary data
PE file parsing with pefile library
Log file analysis: regex pattern matching, timestamp parsing
CSV and JSON output for forensic reporting
Automating evidence collection: file metadata, hash calculation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a script that parses PE file headers, extracts import table, calculates entropy per section, and flags suspicious indicators
setup_requirements: Python with pefile, struct, re modules

review.value: Packs and unpacks binary data. struct.unpack('<HH', data) unpacks two little-endian unsigned shorts. Essential for parsing file headers and binary formats.
pefile module parses PE executables. Access imports via DIRECTORY_ENTRY_IMPORT. Calculate entropy per section to detect packing.

learning_objectives: Understand buffer overflow concepts and memory layout
Write basic exploit payloads in Python
Use pwntools for exploit development automation

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding exploit development gives defenders insight into how attacks work. Writing exploits reinforces memory safety concepts and helps evaluate vulnerability severity.
key_concepts: Memory layout: stack, heap, code, data segments
Buffer overflow: stack smashing, return address overwrite
Shellcode basics: NOP sleds, payload delivery
pwntools: remote(), p32(), p64(), ELF() for exploit scripting
Mitigations: ASLR, DEP/NX, stack canaries, PIE
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploiting a simple buffer overflow in a lab binary using Python and pwntools, then demonstrating how ASLR and stack canaries prevent the same exploit
setup_requirements: Linux VM with GDB and pwntools
GDB with pwndbg or GEF extension

review.value: When input exceeds buffer size on the stack, it overwrites adjacent memory including the return address. Attacker controls the return address to redirect program flow.
Python library for CTF and exploit development. Provides utilities for connection management, payload construction, and binary analysis.

learning_objectives: Implement logging, configuration, and error handling for production tools

lecture_notes.introduction: Moving from scripts to tools requires proper engineering: argument parsing, configuration management, logging, error handling, and packaging for distribution.
key_concepts: argparse for command-line interface design
logging module for structured logging
Configuration files: YAML/JSON for tool configuration
Packaging: setuptools, entry_points, requirements.txt
Testing: pytest for security tool testing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Packaging the tools built throughout the course into a distributable Python package with proper CLI, logging, and documentation

review.value: Scripts that work for you are not tools. Tools have: argument parsing, input validation, error handling, logging, documentation, and tests.
Always use subprocess.run with list arguments instead of shell strings. Validate all inputs. Never hardcode secrets. Use parameterized queries for database access.

questions.question: Write a Python function that takes an IP address and a list of port numbers, performs a TCP connect scan on each port with a 2-second timeout, and returns a dictionary mapping port numbers to their status ('open' or 'closed'). Include error handling.
questions.answer: Create a socket with AF_INET and SOCK_STREAM, set timeout to 2 seconds, use connect_ex() which returns 0 for open ports. Wrap in try/finally to ensure socket.close() is always called. Return dict mapping each port to 'open' or 'closed'.

rubric.key: Correct socket creation and connection logic

questions.question: Explain why constructing shell commands from user input via string formatting is dangerous in a Python security tool. Provide a specific injection example and describe the safe alternative using the subprocess module with list arguments.
questions.answer: String formatting allows command injection through shell metacharacters. An attacker can append additional commands using semicolons or pipes. Safe alternative: use subprocess.run() with a list of arguments and shell=False (default). Each list element is treated as a separate argument, preventing metacharacter interpretation. Additionally, validate all user input matches expected formats before passing to any system command.

rubric.key: Safe alternative with subprocess list form

quizzes.topic: File Analysis, Exploit Development, and Automation

questions.question: Write a Python function using the struct module that reads the first 64 bytes of a file and determines if it is a PE executable by checking for the MZ signature at offset 0 and the PE signature at the offset specified by the e_lfanew field at offset 0x3C.
questions.answer: Open file in binary mode, read 64 bytes. Check bytes 0-1 for b'MZ'. Read 4-byte unsigned int at offset 0x3C to get e_lfanew. Seek to e_lfanew and read 4 bytes, comparing to b'PE\x00\x00'. Handle IOError and struct.error exceptions.

rubric.key: Correct e_lfanew offset reading and PE signature check

questions.question: A stack buffer overflow vulnerability exists in a C program where a 64-byte buffer is allocated on the stack and user input is copied without bounds checking. On a 64-bit Linux system without ASLR, describe the memory layout and explain how you would craft an exploit payload.
questions.answer: Stack layout (high to low): return address (8 bytes), saved RBP (8 bytes), 64-byte buffer. Offset from buffer start to return address = 72 bytes. Payload: 72 bytes of padding followed by the target return address in little-endian 64-bit format. The return address points to shellcode (placed in the buffer with a NOP sled) or a ROP gadget chain. Without ASLR, addresses are static and predictable.

rubric.key: Correct stack layout and offset calculation

major_assignments.title: Security Automation Tool Development
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete security automation tool that solves a real security operations problem. The tool must be packaged for distribution with proper CLI, documentation, and tests.
objectives: Build a functional security tool in Python
deliverables: Complete Python package with source code
README with installation and usage instructions
Test suite with greater than 80 percent code coverage
Demo video showing the tool in action

criteria: Tool solves a real security problem
Functional security tool solving a real problem
Properly packaged and documented for distribution

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of Python programming skills applied to security

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on programming labs building security scripts and tools

midterm_examination.description: Practical coding exam: build a security script from requirements

final_examination.description: Comprehensive practical exam covering all Python security programming topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be discussed. All code must be individually written.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Using library functions and modules is expected. Copying entire solutions from external sources is plagiarism.
course_policies.lab_safety: Security tools developed in this course must only be used within the lab environment. Using tools against unauthorized targets is prohibited.
